front: Arrest in the home: absent consent or exigent circumstances, what does the Fourth Amendment require before police cross the threshold to make an arrest?
back: A warrant — the home gets the Amendment's highest protection, so police may not cross the threshold to arrest without a warrant unless consent or exigent circumstances apply.

front: Payton v. New York — what is the holding on warrantless entry into a suspect's OWN home for a routine felony arrest?
back: Warrantless, nonconsensual entry into a suspect's own home to make a routine felony arrest is presumptively unreasonable.

front: Payton v. New York — what does an arrest warrant founded on probable cause authorize as to the suspect's OWN dwelling?
back: It implicitly carries the limited authority to enter a dwelling in which the suspect lives when there is reason to believe the suspect is within.

front: Payton v. New York — what TWO predicates must be satisfied for an arrest warrant to authorize entry into the suspect's own home?
back: (1) The suspect lives there, and (2) there is reason to believe the suspect is presently within.

front: Payton's "firm line": "the Fourth Amendment has drawn a {{firm line at the entrance to the house}}. Absent exigent circumstances, that threshold may not reasonably be crossed without a warrant."
back: firm line at the entrance to the house

front: Which case held that the Fourth Amendment draws a "firm line at the entrance to the house" that absent exigency may not be crossed without a warrant?
back: Payton v. New York, 445 U.S. 573 (1980).

front: Payton's "reason to believe the suspect is within" — why is the QUANTUM of proof an open circuit split?
back: Payton never defined the showing; one camp reads it as probable cause (that the suspect resides there and is presently inside), another reads it as a lesser standard closer to reasonable, articulable suspicion. It is unresolved at the Supreme Court.

front: For a multi-jurisdiction audience, which standard should you DEFAULT to for Payton's "reason to believe the suspect is within," and why?
back: Default to the higher (probable-cause) showing — it satisfies every circuit, and the articulation costs nothing.

front: Steagald v. United States — what warrant is needed to arrest the subject of an arrest warrant inside a THIRD party's home?
back: A search warrant (absent exigency or consent) — an arrest warrant alone is not enough.

front: Steagald v. United States — WHOSE Fourth Amendment interest does the search-warrant requirement for a third-party home protect?
back: The third-party homeowner's interest — a distinct, independently protected interest; the arrest warrant protects the named suspect, not the homeowner.

front: Under Steagald, who can and who CANNOT invoke the third-party-home rule to suppress?
back: The homeowner can invoke it; the suspect (a guest) cannot, because the protected interest is the homeowner's, not the suspect's.

front: Distinguish Payton from Steagald: how does the warrant requirement change between the suspect's OWN home and a THIRD party's home?
back: Suspect's own home (Payton): an arrest warrant + reason to believe the suspect is within authorizes entry. Third party's home (Steagald): a search warrant is required, because the homeowner's privacy is a separate interest.

front: Police have a valid arrest warrant for a guest staying in someone else's home and enter the home to arrest him without more — which case identifies the violation?
back: Steagald v. United States — they needed a search warrant for the third party's home.

front: United States v. Santana — what is the Fourth Amendment status of a suspect standing in her own doorway/threshold?
back: She is in a public place, so she cannot defeat a public-place arrest by retreating inside.

front: United States v. Santana — what justifies the warrantless entry when a suspect arrested in public retreats inside?
back: Hot pursuit — an arrest lawfully begun in a public place may continue across the threshold into the home.

front: Santana: "a suspect may not defeat an arrest which has been set in motion in a {{public place}} ... by the expedient of escaping to a private place."
back: public place

front: United States v. Santana — does a hot pursuit have to be a long chase to justify warrantless entry?
back: No — a pursuit that ended almost as soon as it began was still a "hot pursuit" sufficient to justify the warrantless entry.

front: Welsh v. Wisconsin — how does the gravity of the offense affect warrantless home entry under the exigency analysis?
back: Offense gravity is a key exigency factor; warrantless home entry for a minor offense should rarely be sanctioned.

front: Which case found a nighttime warrantless home arrest for a noncriminal/minor traffic offense (nighttime DUI, civil-forfeiture offense) unconstitutional?
back: Welsh v. Wisconsin, 466 U.S. 740 (1984).

front: Welsh: "application of the exigent-circumstances exception in the context of a home entry should {{rarely be sanctioned}} when there is probable cause to believe that only a minor offense ... has been committed."
back: rarely be sanctioned

front: Kentucky v. King — do police forfeit the exigency exception merely because their conduct "created" the exigency?
back: No — they do not forfeit it unless they created the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment.

front: Kentucky v. King — state the rule verbatim for when the exigent circumstances rule applies despite police involvement.
back: "The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment."

front: Kentucky v. King — does a lawful knock-and-announce that prompts occupants to destroy evidence disqualify the resulting warrantless entry?
back: No — lawful police conduct that prompts the exigency does not disqualify the entry; only conduct that itself violates (or threatens to violate) the Fourth Amendment does.

front: Lange v. California — does pursuit of a fleeing MISDEMEANOR suspect categorically justify warrantless home entry?
back: No — misdemeanor pursuit does not categorically justify a warrantless home entry; courts make a case-by-case assessment of exigency.

front: Lange: "pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect does not always—that is, {{categorically}}—justify a warrantless entry into a home."
back: categorically

front: Lange v. California — what analytical approach replaces a categorical rule for a fleeing misdemeanant's flight into a home?
back: A case-by-case assessment of exigency (e.g., escape, evidence destruction, danger).

front: Distinguish Santana from Lange: how does Lange cabin Santana's fleeing-suspect/hot-pursuit language?
back: Santana's broad fleeing-suspect language survives for felonies and genuine exigencies, but after Lange it no longer automatically carries MISDEMEANOR pursuits across the threshold — those are judged case by case.

front: Arrest in the home: name the three ways the page says the exigency excusing a home-entry warrant is BOUNDED, and the case for each.
back: It is weakest for minor offenses (Welsh); it is not forfeited merely because lawful police conduct prompted it (King); and misdemeanor pursuit is judged case by case, not categorically (Lange).

front: Why does Payton v. New York matter — what doctrine does it anchor for arrests in the home?
back: It sets the spine of the rule: it establishes the "firm line" at the home's threshold and defines what an arrest warrant authorizes for the suspect's own dwelling (entry on reason to believe the suspect is within).

front: Common pitfall: officers use a valid arrest warrant to enter a third party's home to grab a guest. Why is that wrong?
back: It is a Steagald violation — you need a search warrant for the home, even with a valid arrest warrant for the guest inside.

front: Common pitfall: reading Santana as a blank check for hot pursuit. What does Lange require instead for a fleeing misdemeanant?
back: Chasing a fleeing misdemeanant into a home is not automatically lawful after Lange — you must articulate the actual exigency (escape, evidence loss, danger); the categorical rule applies to felony arrests and genuine exigencies.

front: Common pitfall: assuming any arrest supports home exigency. What does Welsh say for a minor offense?
back: The opposite — for a minor offense, warrantless home entry should "rarely be sanctioned."

front: Common pitfall: believing a "police-created exigency" always poisons the entry. What does Kentucky v. King actually require for forfeiture?
back: Only if the police created the exigency by conduct that itself violated or threatened to violate the Fourth Amendment; lawful knocking that triggers evidence destruction is fine.

front: Common pitfall: treating Payton's "reason to believe" as a hunch. What should an officer articulate instead?
back: In several circuits it means probable cause that the suspect both lives there and is presently inside — articulate both halves with facts behind each.

front: Minnesota v. Olson — does an overnight guest have any Fourth Amendment privacy interest in the host's home?
back: Yes — an overnight guest has his own reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable.

front: Olson: "Olson's status as an {{overnight guest}} is alone enough to show that he had an expectation of privacy in the home that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable."
back: overnight guest

front: Minnesota v. Olson — what does the overnight guest's reasonable expectation of privacy give him procedurally when police make a warrantless entry to arrest him?
back: Standing to object — because he has his own protected privacy interest, a warrantless, nonconsensual entry to seize him implicates the home's full Payton protection and he can move to suppress.

front: Which case holds that an overnight guest's status alone establishes a reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home, giving him Fourth Amendment standing?
back: Minnesota v. Olson, 495 U.S. 91 (1990).

front: Common pitfall: assuming a guest "has no rights" in the host's home. Why is that wrong?
back: Under Olson an overnight guest has his own reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home — even where the homeowner's right (Steagald) is the headline, the guest may also have a privacy interest and standing of his own.

front: Map the three rights-holders in the arrest-in-the-home scenario — who is protected, and what does each require/give?
back: Payton protects the ARRESTEE in his own home (arrest warrant + reason to believe he is within suffices); Steagald protects the THIRD-PARTY homeowner (police need a search warrant for that home); Olson gives the overnight GUEST his own reasonable expectation of privacy (and standing).

front: In the third-party-home arrest scenario, why can't the named suspect invoke Steagald, yet he can still move to suppress?
back: Steagald protects the homeowner's interest, not the suspect's — but the suspect can invoke his own Olson/Payton privacy interest in the place he is staying, so standing tracks the interest you actually hold.
